name: Enkai
description: Enkai, also known as Engai or Ngai, is the supreme God of the Maasai people. Enkai is a singular deity, possessing both masculine and feminine aspects, representing the duality of nature. Enkai is primarily associated with the sky, rain, and fertility, and is considered the provider of all good things. Enkai manifests in two distinct forms: Enkai Narok (the Black God), who is benevolent and brings rain for the pastures, and Enkai Nanyokie (the Red God), who is wrathful and brings drought and famine. The Maasai believe that Enkai resides on Mount Kenya (Ol Donyo Eibor) and is responsible for the changing seasons and the well-being of their cattle, which are central to their culture and survival. Prayers and offerings are made to Enkai to ensure plentiful rains, healthy livestock, and overall prosperity. The relationship between the Maasai and Enkai is one of reciprocal respect and dependence, where the Maasai strive to live in harmony with nature and follow Enkai's will to maintain balance in the world. Enkai's power is absolute and all-encompassing, influencing every aspect of Maasai life, from birth and initiation to marriage and death. The concept of balance between the two aspects of Enkai is crucial, as both the benevolent and wrathful sides are necessary for the natural cycle of life and renewal. Enkai is a complex and multifaceted deity, embodying the Maasai's deep connection to the land and their understanding of the interconnectedness of all things.
subtitle: Supreme God of the Maasai people

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Cattle
keyMyths.description: Enkai gave the Maasai cattle, instructing them to care for them. This established the Maasai as pastoralists.

keyMyths.title: Enkai's Two Sides
keyMyths.description: Enkai has a benevolent side (Black God) that brings rain and a wrathful side (Red God) that brings drought. Both are necessary for balance.

keyMyths.title: Mount Kenya's Significance
keyMyths.description: Mount Kenya is considered Enkai's dwelling place. Prayers and offerings are often directed towards the mountain.
